pattern: A substring can be rearranged into a palindrome exactly when at most one of its digits has an odd count -- so track the parity of each digit's running count as a 10-bit mask and look for an earlier prefix whose mask is identical or differs in only one bit.
hints: A multiset of digits can be shuffled into a palindrome iff at most one digit occurs an odd number of times -- everything else must pair up.

Represent the parity (even/odd) of each of the ten digit counts seen so far as a 10-bit mask, updated one bit at a time as you scan left to right. The mask for a substring is the XOR of the prefix masks at its two ends.

For the current prefix mask, remember the earliest index where you saw that exact mask (zero flipped digits) and also check the ten masks one bit away (exactly one flipped digit) -- the earliest of those matches gives the longest awesome substring ending here.

edge_inputs.description: middle five digits already form an odd-length palindrome after swaps
strictly increasing digits, only single digits are awesome
whole string rearranges into a palindrome
all identical digits, whole string is awesome
one lonely odd digit in the middle of pairs, whole string awesome
leading and trailing zeros count like any other digit

stress.description: random digit strings biased toward a small digit alphabet to create repeats
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    alphabet = '0123'
    s = ''.join(rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(n))
    return [s]
